---
title: "Related Work: Design Science Research"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: IEEEtran
    classoption: [conference]
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Related Work

Our study is situated in the tradition of *Design Science Research* (DSR), the
information-systems (IS) paradigm that produces and evaluates purposeful
*artifacts* --- constructs, models, methods, and instantiations --- as its
primary mode of knowledge creation. We draw on four contributions that together
span the paradigm's foundations, its writing conventions, its theory
apparatus, and its current methodological frontier.

## Foundations and guidelines

@hevner2004design provide the discipline's short-but-canonical reference for
what DSR *is* and how to judge it. They frame IS research as the interplay of
two complementary paradigms --- behavioural science, which *develops and
justifies* theories that explain phenomena, and design science, which *builds
and evaluates* artifacts to solve problems --- and position DSR between an
**environment** (the source of relevance, comprising people, organisations, and
technology) and a **knowledge base** (the source of rigour, comprising
foundations and methodologies). Their central practical contribution is a set
of **seven guidelines**: (1) design as an artifact, (2) problem relevance,
(3) design evaluation, (4) research contributions, (5) research rigour,
(6) design as a search process, and (7) communication of the research to both
technical and managerial audiences. These guidelines remain the default yardstick
against which DSR work --- including this study's artifact --- is assessed.

## A methodology and a nominal structure

Where @hevner2004design specify *criteria*, @peffers2007design specify a
*process*. Their **Design Science Research Methodology (DSRM)** is the de-facto
standard in Wirtschaftsinformatik for structuring both the conduct and the
write-up of DSR. It consists of six activities: (1) problem identification and
motivation, (2) definition of the objectives for a solution, (3) design and
development, (4) demonstration, (5) evaluation, and (6) communication. Crucially,
the model admits four **entry points** --- problem-centred, objective-centred,
design/development-centred, and client/context-initiated --- so that research
starting from an existing artifact or an observed problem can still be reported
within one coherent nominal structure. We adopt this nominal structure to
organise the presentation of our own work.

## Refining the artifact cycle: theory in DSR

@kuechler2012framework refine the artifact-building cycle by asking what kind of
*theory* DSR generates and how design knowledge accumulates. They distinguish two
mid-range theory types. An **Information Systems Design Theory (ISDT)** is
prescriptive --- it states how to build an artifact of a given class to achieve a
goal. A **Design-Relevant Explanatory/Predictive Theory (DREPT)** is descriptive
--- it explains *how and why* the design works by translating kernel theories from
reference disciplines into design-relevant constructs. By coupling ISDT and DREPT
and relating both to Gregor's taxonomy of IS theory through
knowledge-representation, typological, and epistemological lenses, they turn the
single "build-and-evaluate" loop into an explicit engine for cumulative design
knowledge --- clarifying how the artifact contribution of a single study feeds a
growing body of design theory.

## The current frontier: validity in design science

Finally, @larsen2025validity offer a meta-DSR synthesis that consolidates
references to the paradigm's shaping authors and confronts a persistent gap:
validity is neither well understood nor consistently established in design
science. They propose a **Design Science Validity Framework** and accompanying
process model organised around three high-level claim/validity types ---
**criterion**, **causal**, and **context** --- together with finer-grained
subtypes, and they demonstrate the framework on existing DSR examples (and,
reflexively, on itself). This framework gives a vocabulary for stating and
defending exactly *what* an artifact-centred study claims to know, and *how* that
claim is warranted --- a lens we use when arguing the validity of our own
evaluation.

## Positioning of this study {#sec-positioning}

Taken together, these works define the scaffolding for the present study:
@hevner2004design supplies the evaluation criteria, @peffers2007design the
process and the write-up structure, @kuechler2012framework the account of how the
artifact contributes to cumulative design theory, and @larsen2025validity the
contemporary standard for arguing validity. The remainder of this paper follows
the DSRM nominal structure and reports the artifact, its demonstration, and its
evaluation against these standards.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
